title: Project SAFE 1.1
tagline: Supports recovery of Short Sentence inmates and the Drug Rehabilitation Centre inmates with involvement from family members and peer support.
text: Supports recovery of Short Sentence inmates and the Drug Rehabilitation Centre inmates with involvement from family members and peer support.

title: Project SAFE 1.1
tagline: Supports recovery of Short Sentence inmates and the Drug Rehabilitation Centre inmates with involvement from family members and peer support.
text:
Centre-based, Online
Free
Referral required

title: Project SAFE 1.1
tagline: Supports recovery of Short Sentence inmates and the Drug Rehabilitation Centre inmates with involvement from family members and peer support.
text:
Project SAFE 1.1 is a 6 to 9 months programme comprising the following components:

In-care sessions if possible (especially when there is some time before the expected date of release), to build rapport with the inmates

Addiction counselling (individual and group counselling), to acquire relapse prevention and coping skills

Self-help support group, to establish connections with an existing recovering community for long-term sobriety and support

Recovery Guide (Peer Support Specialist), to journey alongside the inmate/ex-offender

Counselling and casework sessions with family members or significant others (if they are agreeable to participate in the programme), to learn how to support in recovery and relapse prevention

Other than the in-care sessions to build rapport with the inmates, all other programme components are conducted after the inmates are released from prison.

title: Project SAFE 1.1
tagline: Supports recovery of Short Sentence inmates and the Drug Rehabilitation Centre inmates with involvement from family members and peer support.
text: Selected Short Sentence inmates and Drug Rehabilitation Centre (DRC) inmates with drug antecedents for the past 3 years

text: Referral by Singapore Prison Service is required.

text:
Singapore Prison Service will refer suitable inmates to the programme. 

WE CARE will contact the family members / significant others (SOs) of these inmates to enroll them in the programme if they are interested.

tagline: Supports recovery of Short Sentence inmates and the Drug Rehabilitation Centre inmates with involvement from family members and peer support.

Supports recovery of Short Sentence inmates and the Drug Rehabilitation Centre inmates with involvement from family members and peer support.

Centre-based, Online
Free
Referral required

Project SAFE 1.1 is a 6 to 9 months programme comprising the following components:

In-care sessions if possible (especially when there is some time before the expected date of release), to build rapport with the inmates

Addiction counselling (individual and group counselling), to acquire relapse prevention and coping skills

Self-help support group, to establish connections with an existing recovering community for long-term sobriety and support

Recovery Guide (Peer Support Specialist), to journey alongside the inmate/ex-offender

Counselling and casework sessions with family members or significant others (if they are agreeable to participate in the programme), to learn how to support in recovery and relapse prevention

Other than the in-care sessions to build rapport with the inmates, all other programme components are conducted after the inmates are released from prison.

Selected Short Sentence inmates and Drug Rehabilitation Centre (DRC) inmates with drug antecedents for the past 3 years

Singapore Prison Service will refer suitable inmates to the programme. 

WE CARE will contact the family members / significant others (SOs) of these inmates to enroll them in the programme if they are interested.

Referral by Singapore Prison Service is required.

text: Pre- and post-marriage support for remarrying couples and their dependent children (below 21) from previous marriage.

title: Remarriage Preparation Programme for Muslim Couples
tagline: Provides pre- and post-marriage support for remarrying couples.
text:
One or both parties are marrying for the second time or more

At least one party is Singapore Citizen or Permanent Resident

One or both parties contemplating marriage or already in a stepfamily have at least one dependent child (below 21 years of age); and

Both parties contemplating marriage or already in a stepfamily, must be a Muslim.

title: Remarriage Preparation Programme for Muslim Couples
tagline: Provides pre- and post-marriage support for remarrying couples.
text: Call, email, visit or through referrals by other agencies to apply.

title: Remarriage Preparation Programme for Muslim Couples
tagline: Provides pre- and post-marriage support for remarrying couples.
text:
Email or call the agency directly to request for more information on the Marriage Preparation Programme (MPP) and marriage support services.

Agency worker will assess and register them for MPP if they meet the criteria

Upon completion of MPP, agency worker will refer to FSCs or SSOs if they have higher risks.

Agency also refers eligible couples to KidStart.

title: Special Education (SPED) School (Autism Spectrum Disorder with intellectual impairment)
tagline: Special Education (SPED) schools provide education for children and youth with disabilities who are of school-going age.
text:
Government and community-funded Special Education (SPED) schools cater to children and youths with moderate-to-severe special educational needs and require more intensive and specialised support in their schooling journey.

Different SPED schools cater to students with different special educational needs.

Visit MOE Special Education (SPED) schools to learn about the support for students with special educational needs and their educational pathways.

title: Special Education (SPED) School (Autism Spectrum Disorder with intellectual impairment)
tagline: Special Education (SPED) schools provide education for children and youth with disabilities who are of school-going age.
text:
These SPED schools offer the customised curriculum for students who have Autism Spectrum Disorder with intellectual impairment.

All schools serve students aged 7 to 18, except:

APSN Chaoyang School (7 to 12 years old)

APSN Katong School (7 to 16 years old)

APSN Tanglin School (13 to 16 years old)

Grace Orchard School (7 to 18 years old)

Metta School (7 to 21 years old)

APSN Delta Senior School (17 to 21 years old)

The six schools mentioned above specifically cater to students who have Autism Spectrum Disorder with mild intellectual disability.

Metta School and APSN Delta Senior School offer Vocational Certification Programmes for students aged 17 to 21.

title: Special Education (SPED) School (Autism Spectrum Disorder with no intellectual impairment)
tagline: Special Education (SPED) schools provide education for children and youth with disabilities who are of school-going age.
text:

Anglo-Chinese School (Academy)

title: Strengthening Families Programme (FAM)
tagline: Provides divorce support and family counselling to all families.
text:
Provides specialised services and programmes for families facing early signs of stress, as well as divorcing and divorced families.

These support services include marriage and divorce support programmes, and a family counselling service.

For Minor couples who are marrying, they are required to attend a complimentary mandatory Marriage Preparation Programme (MPrep - required by law) provided by the agency.

Young couples are also encouraged to attend the MPrep before they marry.

title: Strengthening Families Programme (FAM)
tagline: Provides divorce support and family counselling to all families.
text:
Divorcing and divorced families

Families facing marital, parenting or intrapersonal issues

Individuals/couples below 21 years old need to seek parental or guardian (who is above 21 years old) consent.

Strengthening Families Programme (Fei Yue - Choa Chu Kang) and Strengthening Families Programme (Care Corner - Tampines) also provide marriage support for civil minor couples (either one or both parties under 18 years old), civil young couples (groom aged between 21 and 24, bride 21 & above), and transnational couples

title: Sober Living Framework (SLF)
tagline: Provides crisis intervention, therapy support, and long-term aftercare for persons and families impacted by substance and/or behavioural addictions.
text: Provides crisis intervention, therapy support, and long-term aftercare for persons and families impacted by substance and/or behavioural addictions.

title: Sober Living Framework (SLF)
tagline: Provides crisis intervention, therapy support, and long-term aftercare for persons and families impacted by substance and/or behavioural addictions.
text:
Centre-based, Helpline, Online
Fees payable
Referral not required

title: Sober Living Framework (SLF)
tagline: Provides crisis intervention, therapy support, and long-term aftercare for persons and families impacted by substance and/or behavioural addictions.
text:
The SLF comprises intervention tracks for addicted persons or Significant Other Persons (SOP; includes kin and family members, partner, close friends, guardian) at 5 different stages of needs:

Track 1: Outreach, Education and Awareness – to raise awareness of addictions and advocate for early intervention

Track 2: Relapse Prevention and Extended Care – individual and group counselling, family support, self-help support group

Track 3: Sober Living – for persons in the early remission phase with less than 6 months sobriety from addictive behaviour

Track 4: Maintenance – for persons with at least 6 months sobriety

Track 5: For Family/SOP – individual and group counselling, support group

Not all participants will enter and complete each track in a linear order.

title: Sober Living Framework (SLF)
tagline: Provides crisis intervention, therapy support, and long-term aftercare for persons and families impacted by substance and/or behavioural addictions.
text:
All services are open to Singapore Citizens and persons residing in Singapore who are:

Assessed to have a present or past addiction issue (including drugs, alcohol, compulsive gambling, shoplifting, sex, porn use and gaming), with the expressed desire to seek help or,

Currently impacted by the addiction of a loved one.

For addiction treatment:

Services are provided to all persons aged 14 and above

For family counselling:

Support is provided to individuals aged 10 and above

For outreach programmes:

Conducted for individuals aged 14 and above

Those who are under the age of 16 must be accompanied by a parent, guardian or authorised adult.

title: Sober Living Framework (SLF)
tagline: Provides crisis intervention, therapy support, and long-term aftercare for persons and families impacted by substance and/or behavioural addictions.
text:
You may contact WE CARE for more information. 
Alternatively, WE CARE also accepts referrals through other agencies (e.g. State Courts, MSF, Prisons, family service centres, hospitals, other social service agencies).

title: Sober Living Framework (SLF)
tagline: Provides crisis intervention, therapy support, and long-term aftercare for persons and families impacted by substance and/or behavioural addictions.
text:
The intake and assessment process involves an interview where the person’s motivation is assessed and their readiness, reasons and desire for change are explored.

For persons who are assessed to be ambivalent about stopping their addictive behaviour, brief therapy will be conducted, with the primary goal of engaging with the person such that they will return for help in the future when ready for change.

For persons who are assessed to be highly motivated for change, they will be recommended to seek medical detoxification (if necessary), or directly assigned to the relevant track.

text: Clients can access SOS Hotline calling 1767, a 24-hour hotline manned by trained volunteers and supported by professional staff. title: Sheltered Workshop
tagline: Sheltered Workshops offer employment and/or vocational training to adults with disabilities who do not possess the competencies or skills for open employment.
text: Sheltered Workshops offer employment and/or vocational training to adults with disabilities who do not possess the competencies or skills for open employment.

title: Sheltered Workshop
tagline: Sheltered Workshops offer employment and/or vocational training to adults with disabilities who do not possess the competencies or skills for open employment.
text:
Centre-based
Free
Referral required

title: Sheltered Workshop
tagline: Sheltered Workshops offer employment and/or vocational training to adults with disabilities who do not possess the competencies or skills for open employment.
text:
Sheltered workshops offer the following:

Employment and/or vocational training to adults with disabilities who do not possess the competencies or skills for open employment.

Allow participants to practice in jobs or tasks where the processes are either simple or broken down into simpler steps.

Participants will gain valuable experience at the workshop, thereby improving their prospects for open employment.

title: Sheltered Workshop
tagline: Sheltered Workshops offer employment and/or vocational training to adults with disabilities who do not possess the competencies or skills for open employment.
text:
Applicants should be:

18 years old and above*^#

Singapore citizens of Permanent Residents^^

Have a permanent disability based on any one of the following:

Physical Disability: Requires some assistance with at least 1 of the 6 Activities of Daily Living due to physical impairment

A visual impairment: Moderate visual impairment or worse in the better eye 

A hearing impairment: Moderate hearing loss or worse in the better ear 

Intellectual disability

Autism

Proof of disability must be provided using the Disability Verification Form** completed by a relevant registered healthcare professional.

Applicants should not be suffering from any untreated infectious disease.

*Students of Government and community-funded SPED schools should be encouraged to remain in school until graduation. Parents of SPED graduands may approach their School to discuss the transition plans of the children prior to graduating from School.

^Applicants who are below age 21 will need consent of parents or legal guardians for their application.

#Applicants who are aged 21 and above with no mental capacity, will need the consent of the appointed deputy(s)/donee(s) on behalf of the applicant.

^^For Permanent Residents, at least one immediate family member must be a Singapore Citizen. Immediate family members refer to spouses, parents, children or the applicant, including stepparents and stepchildren.

**From 8 September 2025, the Disability Verification Form (DVF) is the standardised proof of disability required to verify an individual’s disability status when applying for selected MSF disability schemes and services and other selected Government schemes. Applicants can check if their disability status is already verified via their Touchpoints, SupportGoWhere, or call SG Enable at 1800-8585-885

title: Sheltered Workshop
tagline: Sheltered Workshops offer employment and/or vocational training to adults with disabilities who do not possess the competencies or skills for open employment.
text:
Please approach a social service professional from a Touchpoint (e.g. Social Service Agency (SSA), hospitals etc.) for assistance in submitting a Sheltered Workshop application. If you are currently not receiving support from any social service or medical touchpoints, you may approach SG Enable for assistance.

Before meeting your Touchpoints, all applicants are encouraged to check if your disability status has already been verified by logging into SupportGoWhere with your Singpass. If you do not have a verified disability status, the Touchpoint will guide you to the relevant registered healthcare professionals to assist with DVF.

Submission of applications to SG Enable by Social Service Professional (Online or Hardcopy)

Step 1:

All applicants should check if they have a verified disability status by logging into SupportGoWhere with their Singpass. If the applicant already has a verified disability status, a DVF is not required.

Step 2:

If the applicant does not have a verified disability status or has a new disability condition which has not been verified previously, registered Touchpoint Assessor/Admin must submit a copy of the DVF completed by a relevant registered Healthcare Professional together with the AD application.

Step 3:

Registered touchpoints’ Assessor/Admin may submit clients’ AD application and completed DVF online via CorpPass (recommended) or via hardcopy.

Online submission (Recommended)

Log into e-Services using your CorpPass

Hardcopy submission

Download the AD application form, print and send together with the required documents. If required, you may download sections of the AD application form and DVF below for use:

Client Assessment Form (Revised)

Medical Information Form

Declaration Form

Disability Verification Form

title: Sheltered Workshop
tagline: Sheltered Workshops offer employment and/or vocational training to adults with disabilities who do not possess the competencies or skills for open employment.
text:
Sheltered Workshops offer employment and/or vocational training to adults with disabilities who do not possess the competencies or skills for open employment.

Centre-based
Free
Referral required

Sheltered workshops offer the following:

Employment and/or vocational training to adults with disabilities who do not possess the competencies or skills for open employment.

Allow participants to practice in jobs or tasks where the processes are either simple or broken down into simpler steps.

Participants will gain valuable experience at the workshop, thereby improving their prospects for open employment.

Applicants should be:

18 years old and above*^#

Singapore citizens of Permanent Residents^^

Have a permanent disability based on any one of the following:

Physical Disability: Requires some assistance with at least 1 of the 6 Activities of Daily Living due to physical impairment

A visual impairment: Moderate visual impairment or worse in the better eye 

A hearing impairment: Moderate hearing loss or worse in the better ear 

Intellectual disability

Autism

Proof of disability must be provided using the Disability Verification Form** completed by a relevant registered healthcare professional.

Applicants should not be suffering from any untreated infectious disease.

*Students of Government and community-funded SPED schools should be encouraged to remain in school until graduation. Parents of SPED graduands may approach their School to discuss the transition plans of the children prior to graduating from School.

^Applicants who are below age 21 will need consent of parents or legal guardians for their application.

#Applicants who are aged 21 and above with no mental capacity, will need the consent of the appointed deputy(s)/donee(s) on behalf of the applicant.

^^For Permanent Residents, at least one immediate family member must be a Singapore Citizen. Immediate family members refer to spouses, parents, children or the applicant, including stepparents and stepchildren.

**From 8 September 2025, the Disability Verification Form (DVF) is the standardised proof of disability required to verify an individual’s disability status when applying for selected MSF disability schemes and services and other selected Government schemes. Applicants can check if their disability status is already verified via their Touchpoints, SupportGoWhere, or call SG Enable at 1800-8585-885

Upon receiving the referral, SG Enable will contact you to provide information to help you make an informed decision on your choice of service.

Acceptance into the programme at the preferred centre is subject to the availability of vacancies and approval of the centre management.

Please approach a social service professional from a Touchpoint (e.g. Social Service Agency (SSA), hospitals etc.) for assistance in submitting a Sheltered Workshop application. If you are currently not receiving support from any social service or medical touchpoints, you may approach SG Enable for assistance.

Before meeting your Touchpoints, all applicants are encouraged to check if your disability status has already been verified by logging into SupportGoWhere with your Singpass. If you do not have a verified disability status, the Touchpoint will guide you to the relevant registered healthcare professionals to assist with DVF.

Submission of applications to SG Enable by Social Service Professional (Online or Hardcopy)

Step 1:

All applicants should check if they have a verified disability status by logging into SupportGoWhere with their Singpass. If the applicant already has a verified disability status, a DVF is not required.

Step 2:

If the applicant does not have a verified disability status or has a new disability condition which has not been verified previously, registered Touchpoint Assessor/Admin must submit a copy of the DVF completed by a relevant registered Healthcare Professional together with the AD application.

Step 3:

Registered touchpoints’ Assessor/Admin may submit clients’ AD application and completed DVF online via CorpPass (recommended) or via hardcopy.

Online submission (Recommended)

Log into e-Services using your CorpPass

Hardcopy submission

Download the AD application form, print and send together with the required documents. If required, you may download sections of the AD application form and DVF below for use:

Client Assessment Form (Revised)

Medical Information Form

The programme works with the parents and teachers of the identified students which leads to better school outcomes such as lower chronic school absenteeism, premature withdrawal from school and other adolescent risk behaviours.

title: Transit Point @ Margaret Drive (TP@MD)
tagline: Provides short term accommodation and social work intervention to rough sleeping individuals, with 24/7 admission.
text:
Transit Point @ Margaret Drive (TP@MD) is an interim shelter with 24/7 admission, which aims to: 

a) provide immediate safe accommodation for rough sleepers; and 

b) enable rough sleepers to achieve long-term stable housing and equip them for independent living.

title: Transit Point @ Margaret Drive (TP@MD)
tagline: Provides short term accommodation and social work intervention to rough sleeping individuals, with 24/7 admission.
text:
TP@MD admits rough sleepers who meet the following criteria:  

a) Be a Singapore Citizen or Permanent Resident 

b) Aged 18 years and above 

c) Self-declared to be free from serious infectious diseases that may be easily transmitted in a communal setting 

d) Self-declared to not be suffering from serious psychotic disorders, and/or serious behavioural problems, and/or serious medical conditions that require him/her to be under close individual supervision or require nursing care 

e) Self-declared to be non-active substance or alcohol abusers

Transit Point @ Margaret Drive (TP@MD) is an interim shelter with 24/7 admission, which aims to: 

a) provide immediate safe accommodation for rough sleepers; and 

b) enable rough sleepers to achieve long-term stable housing and equip them for independent living.

TP@MD admits rough sleepers who meet the following criteria:  

a) Be a Singapore Citizen or Permanent Resident 

b) Aged 18 years and above 

c) Self-declared to be free from serious infectious diseases that may be easily transmitted in a communal setting 

d) Self-declared to not be suffering from serious psychotic disorders, and/or serious behavioural problems, and/or serious medical conditions that require him/her to be under close individual supervision or require nursing care 

e) Self-declared to be non-active substance or alcohol abusers

Persons who are homeless/sleeping rough are first housed in the Holding Area where their immediate basic needs can be taken care of, followed by an assessment of their suitability for the Stay-in Shelter programme. The Stay-in Shelter Programme will provide social work intervention to address their longer-term needs.

You may contact your nearest Family Service Centre (FSC) or Social Service Office (SSO) for a referral. To find the FSC/SSO in your neighbourhood, call Comcare hotline at 1800 222 0000 or visit https://www.msf.gov.sg/our-services/directories

title: The Scaffold Programme
tagline: Supports Secondary 1 and 2 students, who have been identified to have higher needs, including socio-emotional and behavioural difficulties, at risk of delinquency, and observed to have low school connectedness.
text:
The Scaffold Programme (TSP) is an upstream, early intervention programme that supports lower secondary school students in mainstream schools, who are predisposed to latent risk factors or premature withdrawal from school.

TSP aims to provide quality school social work intervention for students and their ecosystems that lead to improved school outcomes, reduced referrals to downstream programmes, improvement in parent-child relationships and school-student attachment.

title: Transitional Shelter (TS)
tagline: Provide temporary accommodation and social work intervention to homeless families who have exhausted all other means of accommodation.
text:

The TS will only accept applications from FSCs and other community agencies with social work competencies that can assess whether the family/individual meets the admission criteria.

The referring agency will need to send in the application form, social report and supporting documents to TS for assessment.

To find the FSC in your neighbourhood, call Comcare hotline at 1800 222 0000 or visit

Clients must work with the TS on case goals and intervention plans to achieve long-term stable housing eventually.

title: Youth COP
tagline: Aims to foster positive development and strengthen protective factors, empowering youths as crime-prevention leaders to help their communities thrive, stay safe, and build resilience.

text:
Centre-based
Free
Referral required

title: Youth COP
tagline: Aims to foster positive development and strengthen protective factors, empowering youths as crime-prevention leaders to help their communities thrive, stay safe, and build resilience.
text:
Youth COP is a 3-year developmental programme that empowers students to become confident, responsible crime-prevention leaders who help keep their peers and communities safe. In partnership with Neighbourhood Police Centres and selected schools, the programme develops youths into active contributors who serve, protect, and care for their community through meaningful engagement and public education efforts.

 

Through strong collaboration with families, schools, police, and the wider community, Youth COP equips students with leadership skills, crime-prevention knowledge, and opportunities to make a positive impact. As Crime Prevention Ambassadors, participants build a strong sense of identity and self-worth, gain the competencies to navigate life’s challenges, make informed decisions, and foster safer, more caring school and community environments.

title: Youth COP
tagline: Aims to foster positive development and strengthen protective factors, empowering youths as crime-prevention leaders to help their communities thrive, stay safe, and build resilience.
text: The programme supports secondary school students in selected schools, with leadership potential by providing guidance, developmental opportunities, and the knowledge and skills to understand laws and their implications. Youth COP nurtures these young people into positive contributors within their schools and communities, grounded in the belief that all youths grow and thrive when supported with strong relationships, meaningful experiences, and opportunities to realise their potential.

title: Youth COP
tagline: Aims to foster positive development and strengthen protective factors, empowering youths as crime-prevention leaders to help their communities thrive, stay safe, and build resilience.
text:
Students can nominate themselves through the booths in school.

Students can also be nominated for the programme through school personnel (e.g. teachers).

title: Youth COP
tagline: Aims to foster positive development and strengthen protective factors, empowering youths as crime-prevention leaders to help their communities thrive, stay safe, and build resilience.
text:
Programme staff will conduct a selection interview with the nominated student, either through self-nomination or referral by school personnel.

Students’ suitability and commitment for the programme will be assessed within 2–3 weeks of referral or sign-up.

If a student is deemed unsuitable or unable to commit, they will receive a letter explaining the decision and, where appropriate, be referred to alternative support services based on the social worker’s recommendations.

Free
Referral required

Youth COP is a 3-year developmental programme that empowers students to become confident, responsible crime-prevention leaders who help keep their peers and communities safe. In partnership with Neighbourhood Police Centres and selected schools, the programme develops youths into active contributors who serve, protect, and care for their community through meaningful engagement and public education efforts.

 

Through strong collaboration with families, schools, police, and the wider community, Youth COP equips students with leadership skills, crime-prevention knowledge, and opportunities to make a positive impact. As Crime Prevention Ambassadors, participants build a strong sense of identity and self-worth, gain the competencies to navigate life’s challenges, make informed decisions, and foster safer, more caring school and community environments.

The programme supports secondary school students in selected schools, with leadership potential by providing guidance, developmental opportunities, and the knowledge and skills to understand laws and their implications. Youth COP nurtures these young people into positive contributors within their schools and communities, grounded in the belief that all youths grow and thrive when supported with strong relationships, meaningful experiences, and opportunities to realise their potential.

Programme staff will conduct a selection interview with the nominated student, either through self-nomination or referral by school personnel.

Students’ suitability and commitment for the programme will be assessed within 2–3 weeks of referral or sign-up.

If a student is deemed unsuitable or unable to commit, they will receive a letter explaining the decision and, where appropriate, be referred to alternative support services based on the social worker’s recommendations.

Students can nominate themselves through the booths in school.

Students can also be nominated for the programme through school personnel (e.g. teachers).

title: YMCA Vocational and Soft Skills Programme (VaSSP)
tagline: Aims to equip out-of-school youths and youths-at-risk with job competency as well as specific vocational skills.
text:
The YMCA Vocational and Soft Skills Programme (VaSSP), also known as Project Bridge, aims to equip out-of-school youths and youths-at-risk of dropping out (who are absent from school for a long period) with essential soft skills and vocational expertise, enabling them to enhance their employability to integrate into the workforce or to reintegrate to education.

Conducted over 6 months, the programme consists to three phases: Core Training, Vocational Training, and Monitoring Phase.

Service users engage in soft skills training, covering crucial areas such as goal setting, resume writing, interview skills, and communication and conflict resolution. Subsequently, youths can select one out of 14 vocational tracks and receive certified training in the chosen industry. Thereafter, YMCA staff will continue to monitor the service users for a period of time before completion of the programme by supporting them in aspects such as career counselling or academic support in the service users' journey to either obtain employment or reintegrate back into education.

title: The GRIT Academy
tagline: Mentoring programme for youths-at-risk in Jurong.
text: The GRIT Academy (TGA) aims to provide individual mentoring support for youths-at-risk aged between 11 to 17 years old residing or studying in schools in Jurong; and serve as the primary relationship holder to follow through with the youths as they transit to other youth programmes.

title: The GRIT Academy
tagline: Mentoring programme for youths-at-risk in Jurong.
text:
The target clientele is youths-at-risk aged between 11 to 17 years old residing or studying in schools in Jurong (i.e. Jurong West, Jurong East and Taman Jurong). 

Applicants must fulfil at least one of the following criteria to be eligible for the programme: 

(a) Youths who lack meaningful engagements such as those not attending Co-Curricular Activities, as well as youths who often loiter at public areas such as playgrounds and void decks. 

(b) Youths with underprivileged background such as youths from low-income families, students receiving the MOE Financial Assistance Scheme and youths staying in rental flats. 

(c) Youths exhibiting at-risk behaviour such as poor affiliation to schools, minor behavioural issues, socio-emotional issues and/or peer issues.

title: Youth Integrated Team (YIT)
tagline: Provides community-based mental health assessment, therapeutic intervention, case management for youths with mild mental health conditions while monitoring and checking in with the youth before, during and after intervention has been provided.
text:
You can approach over 100 community mental health teams in the community islandwide such as YIT, providing the following support:

 Provide mental health assessments and support youths in understanding and identifying their care needs.

Provide coordinated person-centric care intervention and support for youths and their caregivers through providing assessments, therapeutic intervention and case management.

Engage community and healthcare partners to build a supportive environment for youths with mental health conditions through active collaboration and co-management of cases.

Facilitate the formation of supportive relationships with families and peers in the recovery journey of youths.

 

These community mental health teams are developed under the Community Mental Health Masterplan driven by the Agency for Integrated Care (AIC), together with the Ministry of Health (MOH). The teams are fully funded by the government and there is no out-of-pocket cost.

title: Youth GO! Programme
tagline: An outreach, engagement and intervention programme for at-risk youth (aged 12-21) that helps them stay focused in their studies/work, remain crime-free, develop problem-solving skills and be resilient.
text:
Youth GO! is an outreach, engagement and intervention programme that supports at-risk youth.

Its objectives are to help at-risk youth be meaningfully engaged in their studies or work, stay crime-free, develop problem-solving skills and be resilient individuals.

title: Youth GO! Programme
tagline: An outreach, engagement and intervention programme for at-risk youth (aged 12-21) that helps them stay focused in their studies/work, remain crime-free, develop problem-solving skills and be resilient.
text:
At-risk youth who:

are Singapore Citizens/Permanent Residents (PR).

are between 12 - 21 years old.

are out-of-school and not engaged in meaningful work, or

have completed the Guidance Programme (GP), Enhanced Step Up (ESU), Streetwise Programme (SWP), Youth Enhanced Supervision (YES) and/or Probation but require further support upon completion of these programmes, or

are school students with high absenteeism and/or who opted or dropped out of the ESU.

text:
Referrals can be made by contacting the respective Youth GO! service provider according to the region of the youth's residential address or the hotspot area.

Please refer to Youth GO! Programme webpage for information on the regions served by the different service providers, and their contact information.

title: Youth GO! Programme
tagline: An outreach, engagement and intervention programme for at-risk youth (aged 12-21) that helps them stay focused in their studies/work, remain crime-free, develop problem-solving skills and be resilient.
text:
For hotspot referrals, Youth GO! workers will work towards befriending the youth to assess if they fit the criteria of the target clientele.

Where referrals are successful, Youth GO! workers will continue to engage the youth and conduct casework and intervention through positive engagement activities and case counselling sessions.

text:
An outreach, engagement and intervention programme for at-risk youth (aged 12-21) that helps them stay focused in their studies/work, remain crime-free, develop problem-solving skills and be resilient.

Centre-based
Free
Referral required

Youth GO! is an outreach, engagement and intervention programme that supports at-risk youth.

Its objectives are to help at-risk youth be meaningfully engaged in their studies or work, stay crime-free, develop problem-solving skills and be resilient individuals.

At-risk youth who:

are Singapore Citizens/Permanent Residents (PR).

are between 12 - 21 years old.

are out-of-school and not engaged in meaningful work, or

have completed the Guidance Programme (GP), Enhanced Step Up (ESU), Streetwise Programme (SWP), Youth Enhanced Supervision (YES) and/or Probation but require further support upon completion of these programmes, or

are school students with high absenteeism and/or who opted or dropped out of the ESU.

For hotspot referrals, Youth GO! workers will work towards befriending the youth to assess if they fit the criteria of the target clientele.

Where referrals are successful, Youth GO! workers will continue to engage the youth and conduct casework and intervention through positive engagement activities and case counselling sessions.

Referrals can be made by contacting the respective Youth GO! service provider according to the region of the youth's residential address or the hotspot area.

Please refer to Youth GO! Programme webpage for information on the regions served by the different service providers, and their contact information.
